blocks.content: df.explain() prints the physical execution plan Spark will run. Reading plans is the difference between a junior engineer who guesses at performance issues and a senior engineer who diagnoses them in 60 seconds. This chapter teaches you to read plans fluently.

rows: Quick check — is this a broadcast join? Shuffles?
Nicely indented physical plan with stats
Physical plan with CBO cost estimates
When CBO is enabled — check estimated row counts

blocks.content: Plans are read bottom-up (leaves = input, root = final output). Indentation shows the tree structure.

# Sample query
df.filter(col('salary') > 80000) \
  .join(broadcast(df_dept), 'dept_id') \
  .groupBy('dept_name') \
  .agg(avg('salary').alias('avg_sal')) \
  .explain(mode='formatted')

# Physical plan (read bottom-up):
# == Physical Plan ==
# *(3) HashAggregate(key=[dept_name], functions=[avg(salary)])
# +- Exchange hashpartitioning(dept_name, 200)          <- SHUFFLE 1 (for groupBy)
#    +- *(2) HashAggregate(key=[dept_name], functions=[partial_avg(salary)])
#       +- *(2) BroadcastHashJoin [dept_id], Inner, BuildRight
#          :- *(2) Filter (salary > 80000)              <- predicates pushed below join
#          :  +- *(2) FileScan parquet [salary, dept_id]
#          +- BroadcastExchange                         <- small table broadcast, not shuffled
#             +- *(1) FileScan parquet [dept_id, dept_name]

rows: Reading Parquet files, columns listed = column pruning in effect
Predicate applied — if below join, pushdown worked
SHUFFLE — rows redistributed by hash(col) into N partitions
SHUFFLE — orderBy() range-partition for global sort
Zero-shuffle join — small table in memory
First phase aggregate — local to executor, before shuffle
Second phase aggregate — after shuffle, computes final result
Whole-stage codegen stage N — compiled to one JVM method

sections.title: What to Look For in Plans

blocks.content: When reviewing a query plan, ask these questions in order:

rows: Each Exchange = full network transfer. Minimize them.
Filter nodes should be below Join nodes and as close to FileScan as possible
Move filter() calls earlier in your code
FileScan should list only your needed columns
Check for select(*) — avoid with wide tables
Is the small table within broadcast threshold? Explicit broadcast()?
Should see HashAggregate (partial) BEFORE Exchange for groupBy
If missing, Spark is shuffling all rows before aggregating
UDFs without codegen show as non-prefixed nodes or ArrowEvalPython
Replace UDF with built-in or pandas_udf

blocks.content:
The 30-second plan review routine (MAANG standard):
1. Count Exchange nodes → minimize shuffles
2. Find Filter positions → should be at/near FileScan
3. Check join type → BroadcastHashJoin preferred
4. Look for ArrowEvalPython / EvalPython → UDF warning
5. Check FileScan column list → column pruning active?

rune_cards.definition: Plan node representing a shuffle. Exchange hashpartitioning(col, N) = shuffle by key. Count Exchange nodes to count shuffles in your query.
First phase of two-phase aggregation. Runs locally on each partition before shuffling — reduces shuffle data. If this is missing, Spark shuffled all rows before aggregating (expensive).
Parquet scan with column pruning. Only listed columns are read from disk. Wide scans (many columns) are a sign of missing select() early in the pipeline.
Small table is broadcast — sent to all executors. Leads to BroadcastHashJoin. No shuffle for this side of the join.
Plan node for a Python UDF (EvalPython = row-by-row Pickle; ArrowEvalPython = pandas_udf). Either breaks whole-stage codegen and adds Python serialization.

quiz.question: You see this in a physical plan: Exchange hashpartitioning(user_id, 200) appearing 3 times. What does this mean and what should you investigate?
options: The query has 3 shuffles on user_id. Investigate if any can be eliminated with broadcast joins or by caching intermediate results
The query uses 3 partitions per executor — this is efficient
Spark is reading 3 separate Parquet files with 200 row groups each
This is normal — Catalyst always generates 3 Exchange nodes for correctness
quiz.explanation: 3 Exchange nodes on the same user_id column means 3 separate shuffles of the same data. First check if any join can be broadcast instead. Then check if an intermediate result is reused — if so, cache() it after the first shuffle so subsequent operations read from cache instead of reshuffling raw data.

quiz.question: You expected column pruning but the plan shows: FileScan parquet [id, name, salary, dept, hire_date, region, manager_id, level, ...] (all 30 columns). What went wrong?
quiz.code:
df = spark.read.parquet('s3://bucket/employees/')
df2 = df.withColumn('bonus', col('salary') * 0.1)
result = df2.select('id', 'name', 'bonus')
options: select() only applies to the final output, not to the FileScan — you need a separate .select() before withColumn()
Catalyst should prune columns — this is a Spark bug and can't be fixed
The filter for column pruning needs col() syntax, not string column names
withColumn('bonus', col('salary') * 0.1) reads all columns because Spark doesn't know which columns bonus depends on
quiz.explanation: Catalyst DOES propagate column pruning through the full plan — in this case it should only read id, name, salary (needed for bonus computation). If you're seeing all 30 columns, double-check: is there a df.cache() or df.rdd call somewhere that forces a full scan? Or check Spark version — older versions had pruning bugs. In modern Spark 3.x this plan should show only 3 columns.
